era_context: The late 1920s mark the interwar period between World War I and World War II, characterized by fragile international optimism, rapid industrialization, ideological polarization between liberal democracy, communism, and rising fascism, and a global economic expansion that would collapse catastrophically in 1929. In China, the Nationalist revolution was reshaping the political order; in the Soviet Union, Stalin was consolidating total power and launching forced industrialization; colonial empires in Africa and Asia remained largely intact but faced growing nationalist movements; and in the West, cultural modernism, mass media, and consumer capitalism were transforming daily life.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa outside colonial administrative records
Central Asia under early Soviet control
Pacific Island societies outside colonial documentation
Indigenous communities in the Americas and Australasia whose histories were poorly recorded by colonial authorities

events.title: Alexander Fleming discovers penicillin
events.description: In September 1928, Alexander Fleming at St. Mary's Hospital, London, observed that a Penicillium mould contaminating a staphylococcus culture plate had killed surrounding bacteria. He published his findings in 1929, identifying the antibacterial substance as 'penicillin.' The discovery would not be developed into a usable drug until the work of Howard Florey and Ernst Boris Chain in the early 1940s, but it ultimately launched the antibiotic revolution that transformed medicine and saved hundreds of millions of lives. Fleming's observation was partly serendipitous — he noticed the contamination upon returning from holiday — but his bacteriological training enabled him to recognize its significance.

sources.name: Fleming, A. 'On the Antibacterial Action of Cultures of a Penicillium,' British Journal of Experimental Pathology, 1929
Wainwright, M. 'Fleming's Discovery of Penicillin,' various historiographic analyses

events.certainty_note: Fleming's own published paper and laboratory notebooks confirm the discovery. The exact date of initial observation is conventionally given as September 3 or 28, 1928, with some ambiguity about the precise day he first noticed the mould.

events.title: Kellogg-Briand Pact signed in Paris
events.description: On August 27, 1928, fifteen nations initially signed the General Treaty for Renunciation of War as an Instrument of National Policy (Kellogg-Briand Pact) in Paris, eventually joined by sixty-two states. Named for U.S. Secretary of State Frank Kellogg and French Foreign Minister Aristide Briand, the treaty formally outlawed aggressive war. While it represented the high-water mark of interwar idealism and international legal ambition, it contained no enforcement mechanism and proved entirely unable to prevent the wars of the 1930s and 1940s. Nonetheless, its legal principle — that aggressive war is illegal — became foundational to the post-WWII international order and the Nuremberg Trials.
key_figures: Frank B. Kellogg (U.S. Secretary of State)

sources.name: Text of the General Treaty for Renunciation of War, August 27, 1928
Hathaway, Oona and Scott Shapiro, 'The Internationalists' (2017)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text and signing ceremony extensively documented by all signatory governments and press.

events.title: Chinese Nationalists capture Beijing; nominal reunification of China
events.description: In June 1928, the National Revolutionary Army under Chiang Kai-shek completed the Northern Expedition by capturing Beijing from the Fengtian warlord clique. Zhang Zuolin, the Manchurian warlord who controlled Beijing, was assassinated by Japanese Kwantung Army officers on June 4 when his train was bombed at Huanggutun near Mukden. His son Zhang Xueliang subsequently pledged allegiance to the Nationalist government in December ('Northeast Flag Replacement'), giving the Kuomintang nominal control over all of China. In reality, vast regions remained under semi-autonomous warlord control, the Communists maintained rural bases, and Japanese influence in Manchuria was growing.
key_figures: Komoto Daisaku (Japanese colonel who planned the assassination)

sources.name: Contemporary diplomatic cables and press reports from Nanjing, Beijing, and Tokyo
Eastman, Lloyd, 'The Abortive Revolution: China Under Nationalist Rule, 1927–1937'

events.certainty_note: Well-documented by multiple governments, military records, and press coverage. The Huanggutun incident was covered up by the Japanese government at the time but is now fully confirmed.

events.title: Soviet Union launches First Five-Year Plan
events.description: In October 1928, the Soviet Union under Joseph Stalin inaugurated the First Five-Year Plan (Pyatiletka), a crash program of forced industrialization and agricultural collectivization. The plan prioritized heavy industry — steel, coal, machinery, and electrification — with fantastically ambitious production targets. It marked the definitive end of the New Economic Policy (NEP) and the beginning of command-economy central planning that would define the Soviet system. The plan's implementation involved immense human suffering, particularly through forced collectivization of agriculture, which would lead to the catastrophic famine of 1932–33. Soviet industrial output did increase dramatically, though official statistics were heavily inflated.
key_figures: Nikolai Bukharin (opponent of rapid industrialization, increasingly marginalized)

sources.name: Soviet government decrees and Gosplan documents, 1928
Davies, R.W., 'The Soviet Economy in Turmoil, 1929–1930' (vol. 3 of The Industrialisation of Soviet Russia)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in Soviet archives and contemporary foreign diplomatic reports. Implementation details and actual outcomes are subject to scholarly debate due to falsified Soviet statistics.

events.title: Herbert Hoover elected President of the United States
events.description: On November 6, 1928, Republican Herbert Hoover defeated Democrat Al Smith in a landslide presidential election, winning 444 electoral votes to Smith's 87. Hoover campaigned on continued prosperity and his reputation as an efficient administrator and humanitarian (from his post-WWI food relief work). Smith's Roman Catholic faith was a significant factor in the campaign, generating intense anti-Catholic prejudice particularly in the traditionally Democratic South, several states of which voted Republican. Hoover entered office with enormous optimism; within months, the Great Depression would define and destroy his presidency.
key_figures: Herbert Hoover (Republican candidate, former Secretary of Commerce)
Al Smith (Democratic candidate, Governor of New York)

sources.name: Official election returns; contemporary press coverage (New York Times, etc.)
Leuchtenburg, William, 'The Perils of Prosperity, 1914–1932'

events.certainty_note: Election results are definitively documented.

events.title: Equal Franchise Act grants women full suffrage in the United Kingdom
events.description: On July 2, 1928, the Equal Franchise Act (also called the 'Equal Vote Act') received Royal Assent, extending voting rights to all women over 21 on the same terms as men. The 1918 Representation of the People Act had granted the vote only to women over 30 who met certain property qualifications. The 1928 Act added approximately 5.2 million women to the electoral rolls, making women a majority of the British electorate for the first time. Emmeline Pankhurst, the iconic suffragette leader, died on June 14, just weeks before the Act passed.
key_figures: William Joynson-Hicks (Home Secretary who introduced the bill)

sources.name: Equal Franchise Act 1928 (18 & 19 Geo. 5 c. 12), Parliamentary records
Pugh, Martin, 'Women and the Women's Movement in Britain, 1914–1999'

events.certainty_note: Parliamentary legislation with full documentary record.

events.title: Amelia Earhart becomes first woman to fly across the Atlantic
events.region: Transatlantic (Trepassey, Newfoundland to Burry Port, Wales)
events.description: On June 17–18, 1928, Amelia Earhart became the first woman to fly across the Atlantic Ocean, traveling from Trepassey, Newfoundland, to Burry Port, Wales, in approximately 20 hours and 40 minutes aboard the Fokker F.VIIb/3m 'Friendship.' Earhart served as a passenger and flight log keeper; the plane was piloted by Wilmer Stultz with mechanic Louis Gordon. Despite her largely passive role on this flight, the achievement made Earhart an instant international celebrity and symbol of women's capabilities in aviation. She would later make a solo transatlantic flight in 1932.

sources.name: Earhart, Amelia, '20 Hrs., 40 Min.' (1928, published account)
Contemporary press coverage (New York Times, etc.)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by press coverage and Earhart's own published account.

events.title: Hirohito formally enthroned as Emperor of Japan
events.description: On November 10, 1928, Emperor Hirohito (Shōwa) underwent the formal enthronement ceremony (Sokui no Rei) at the Kyoto Imperial Palace, followed by the Great Thanksgiving Ceremony (Daijōsai) on November 14–15. Although Hirohito had been regent since 1921 and had acceded to the throne upon his father Taishō's death in December 1926, the elaborate enthronement ceremonies were held after the customary mourning period. The ceremonies reinforced the sacral character of the imperial institution at a time when Japan was moving toward increasing militarism and imperial expansion.

sources.name: Official Japanese government records of enthronement ceremonies; contemporary press coverage
Bix, Herbert, 'Hirohito and the Making of Modern Japan' (2000)

events.certainty_note: Meticulously documented by Japanese government and international press.

events.title: Steamboat Willie premieres; Mickey Mouse debuts
events.description: On November 18, 1928, Walt Disney's animated short film 'Steamboat Willie' premiered at the Colony Theater in New York City. While not technically the first Mickey Mouse cartoon produced (that was 'Plane Crazy'), it was the first widely distributed and the first Disney cartoon with fully synchronized sound throughout, making innovative use of the post-sound film technology. The film launched Mickey Mouse as a cultural icon and established the Walt Disney Studio as a major force in entertainment. Mickey Mouse would become one of the most recognized fictional characters in the world.
key_figures: Walt Disney (producer, voice of Mickey Mouse)

sources.name: Disney studio records; contemporary trade press (Variety, etc.)
Gabler, Neal, 'Walt Disney: The Triumph of the American Imagination' (2006)

events.certainty_note: Premiere date and circumstances well-documented in trade press and studio records.

events.title: Muslim Brotherhood founded in Egypt
events.description: In March 1928, Hassan al-Banna, a 22-year-old schoolteacher, founded the Society of the Muslim Brothers (al-Ikhwan al-Muslimun) in Ismailia, a city in the Suez Canal zone dominated by the British-controlled Suez Canal Company. Initially a religious and social welfare organization promoting Islamic moral reform and education, the Brotherhood grew rapidly and became the most influential Islamist political movement of the 20th century. Al-Banna's ideology combined revivalist Islam with anti-colonial nationalism and a critique of Western cultural influence. The movement would profoundly shape political Islam across the Middle East and beyond.

sources.name: Al-Banna's memoirs and early Brotherhood publications
Mitchell, Richard P., 'The Society of the Muslim Brothers' (1969)
Lia, Brynjar, 'The Society of the Muslim Brothers in Egypt' (1998)

events.certainty_note: Founding date of March 1928 is consistently attested in al-Banna's own writings and Brotherhood organizational history. The exact day is sometimes given as March 22 but this is less certain.

events.title: Sixth National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party held in Moscow
events.description: From June 18 to July 11, 1928, the Chinese Communist Party held its Sixth National Congress — uniquely, in Moscow rather than China, due to the severe repression following the 1927 Shanghai massacre and the collapse of the first KMT-CCP united front. The Congress, attended by approximately 142 delegates, assessed the catastrophic defeats of 1927, adopted a new program emphasizing agrarian revolution and Soviet-style organization, and elected Xiang Zhongfa as General Secretary (though real power lay with the Comintern advisors and figures like Li Lisan and Zhou Enlai). Mao Zedong did not attend, being engaged in rural base-building in Jinggangshan.

sources.name: CCP Sixth Congress documents and resolutions (available in CCP documentary collections)
Saich, Tony, 'The Rise to Power of the Chinese Communist Party' (1996)

events.certainty_note: Congress proceedings documented in party records and Comintern archives.

events.title: Ahmed Zogu declares himself King Zog I of Albania
events.description: On September 1, 1928, President Ahmed Zogu proclaimed Albania a monarchy and crowned himself King Zog I. Having already ruled as president since 1925 (and as prime minister before that), Zog formalized his authoritarian control through a new monarchical constitution approved by a compliant constituent assembly. His rule was heavily dependent on Italian financial support and political backing from Mussolini's government, which used Albania as a vehicle for Italian influence in the Balkans. Zog pursued modernization programs including legal reform (banning blood feuds, promoting secular education) while maintaining autocratic control.
key_figures: Ahmed Zogu / King Zog I (self-proclaimed monarch of Albania)

sources.name: Albanian constitutional documents, 1928; contemporary diplomatic reports
Fischer, Bernd Jürgen, 'King Zog and the Struggle for Stability in Albania' (1984)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in Albanian and international sources.

events.title: Margaret Mead publishes 'Coming of Age in Samoa'
events.description: In 1928, anthropologist Margaret Mead published 'Coming of Age in Samoa,' based on fieldwork conducted in 1925–26 among adolescent girls in Ta'ū, American Samoa. The book argued that Samoan adolescence was relatively stress-free compared to Western experience, suggesting that adolescent turmoil was culturally rather than biologically determined. It became one of the best-selling anthropology books ever written and profoundly influenced the nature-nurture debate in social science, bolstering the cultural determinist position associated with her mentor Franz Boas. The book's findings have been intensely contested, most notably by Derek Freeman beginning in the 1980s.
key_figures: Franz Boas (her mentor at Columbia University)

sources.name: Mead, Margaret, 'Coming of Age in Samoa' (1928)
Freeman, Derek, 'Margaret Mead and Samoa' (1983, critical reassessment)

events.certainty_note: Publication date confirmed. The accuracy of Mead's ethnographic findings remains disputed (see disconfirming evidence section).

events.title: Amsterdam Summer Olympic Games
events.description: The 1928 Summer Olympics were held in Amsterdam from July 28 to August 12, with 46 nations and approximately 2,883 athletes participating. These games were notable as the first in which women were allowed to compete in track and field events (though only five events), and the first to feature a standardized Olympic flame burning throughout the games. The United States led the medal count. The games also saw controversy when several women collapsed after the 800-meter race, leading the IOC to ban women's races longer than 200 meters — a ban not fully reversed until 1960.
key_figures: Lina Radke (German, first women's 800m gold medalist)

sources.name: Official Report of the 1928 Amsterdam Olympic Games
IOC historical records

events.certainty_note: Official Olympic records provide comprehensive documentation.

events.title: António de Oliveira Salazar appointed Finance Minister; rise to power in Portugal
events.description: On April 27, 1928, António de Oliveira Salazar, a professor of economics at the University of Coimbra, was appointed Finance Minister by the military dictatorship that had ruled Portugal since the 1926 coup. Salazar accepted only on condition of full control over all government spending. His success in balancing the budget and stabilizing the currency gave him enormous political authority, which he leveraged to become Prime Minister in 1932 and architect of the Estado Novo, a corporatist authoritarian regime that would endure until 1974. His appointment marks the effective beginning of Europe's longest-lasting authoritarian regime of the 20th century.

sources.name: Portuguese government records and contemporary press
Gallagher, Tom, 'Portugal: A Twentieth-Century Interpretation' (1983)

events.certainty_note: Government appointment records and press coverage confirm the date and circumstances.

events.title: Jinan Incident: Japanese-Chinese military clash in Shandong
events.description: On May 3, 1928, Japanese troops clashed with Chinese Nationalist forces in Jinan (Tsinan), the capital of Shandong province, as the Northern Expedition advanced northward. Japan had deployed troops ostensibly to protect Japanese nationals and interests in Shandong. The incident resulted in several thousand Chinese civilian and military casualties and the killing of the Chinese diplomat Cai Gongshi. The Jinan Incident inflamed Chinese anti-Japanese sentiment, foreshadowed the larger Sino-Japanese conflicts to come, and demonstrated Japan's willingness to use military force to protect its continental interests.

sources.name: Chinese and Japanese diplomatic correspondence; contemporary press
Jordan, Donald, 'China's Trial by Fire: The Shanghai War of 1932' and related scholarship on Sino-Japanese tensions

events.certainty_note: Documented by both Chinese and Japanese sources, though casualty figures and responsibility are disputed between the two national historiographies.

events.title: Stalin moves against the Right Opposition; Shakhty Trial
events.description: Throughout 1928, Joseph Stalin maneuvered against his former allies in the so-called Right Opposition — Nikolai Bukharin, Alexei Rykov, and Mikhail Tomsky — who opposed the abandonment of the New Economic Policy and the turn toward forced collectivization and rapid industrialization. Having already defeated the Left Opposition (Trotsky, Zinoviev, Kamenev) by 1927, Stalin now adopted much of the Left's economic program while destroying the Right politically. The 'Shakhty Trial' of May–July 1928, in which mining engineers were accused of industrial sabotage and wrecking, signaled the new climate of terror and the political use of show trials.
key_figures: Alexei Rykov (Chairman of the Council of People's Commissars)

sources.name: Soviet press coverage of Shakhty Trial; Bukharin's private notes to Kamenev (later published)
Cohen, Stephen F., 'Bukharin and the Bolshevik Revolution' (1973)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Soviet archives, personal correspondence, and diplomatic reports. Internal party dynamics became clearer after archival openings in the 1990s.

events.title: Turkey adopts Latin alphabet, replacing Arabic script
events.description: On November 1, 1928, the Turkish Grand National Assembly passed the Law on the Adoption and Implementation of Turkish Letters, replacing the Arabic script used for Ottoman Turkish with a modified Latin alphabet. The law, a centerpiece of Mustafa Kemal Atatürk's sweeping secularization and Westernization reforms, was implemented with remarkable speed — the new alphabet became mandatory in all public communications by January 1, 1929. The reform effectively severed literate access to the Ottoman textual heritage for subsequent generations while dramatically improving literacy rates (from roughly 10% to much higher levels within a decade), as the Latin script was far better suited to Turkish phonology.

sources.name: Law No. 1353, Turkish Grand National Assembly records, November 1, 1928
Lewis, Geoffrey, 'The Turkish Language Reform: A Catastrophic Success' (1999)

events.certainty_note: Legislative record definitively dated. Implementation timeline documented in government records.

events.title: Raman effect discovered in India
events.description: On February 28, 1928, Indian physicist C.V. Raman and his collaborator K.S. Krishnan at the Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science in Calcutta observed the inelastic scattering of light by molecules, demonstrating that scattered light contains frequencies different from the incident light. This phenomenon, soon called the 'Raman effect,' provided a powerful spectroscopic tool for studying molecular structure and became foundational to modern physics and chemistry. Raman received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1930 — the first Asian to win a Nobel Prize in science. February 28 is celebrated as National Science Day in India.

sources.name: Raman, C.V. and K.S. Krishnan, 'A New Type of Secondary Radiation,' Nature, March 31, 1928
Singh, Rajinder, 'C.V. Raman and the Discovery of the Raman Effect,' Physics in Perspective (2002)

events.certainty_note: Published in Nature with precise experimental records. February 28 date based on Raman's own account of the key observation.

events.title: Chaco War tensions escalate between Bolivia and Paraguay
events.description: On December 6, 1928, Paraguayan forces attacked the Bolivian fort of Vanguardia in the disputed Gran Chaco region, dramatically escalating tensions that had been building for years over this sparsely populated but potentially oil-rich territory. Bolivia retaliated by seizing two Paraguayan forts. Although a fragile peace was temporarily restored through international mediation, the incident was a clear precursor to the full-scale Chaco War (1932–1935), the bloodiest interstate conflict in 20th-century South America. The underlying dispute involved territorial claims dating to the colonial era, exacerbated by rumored oil deposits and the involvement of Standard Oil and Royal Dutch Shell.

sources.name: Diplomatic correspondence between Bolivia, Paraguay, and mediating nations; League of Nations records
Farcau, Bruce, 'The Chaco War: Bolivia and Paraguay, 1932–1935' (1996)

events.certainty_note: Military engagements and diplomatic response documented in multiple national archives.

events.title: Radcliffe-Brown appointed to first chair in social anthropology at Sydney
events.description: In 1928, A.R. Radcliffe-Brown was appointed to the newly created chair of anthropology at the University of Sydney, where he established a department that became enormously influential in Pacific and Aboriginal Australian ethnography. That same year, the Australian government conducted a major policy conference on Aboriginal affairs (the initial Aboriginal Protection conferences), while anthropological fieldwork in the region was documenting Indigenous cultures under intense pressure from colonial dispossession. The year also saw significant research expeditions in the Australian interior studying Aboriginal peoples, including work that would inform later debates about land rights.

sources.name: University of Sydney appointment records; Radcliffe-Brown's published work from this period
Stocking, George, 'After Tylor: British Social Anthropology 1888–1951' (1995)

events.certainty_note: University appointment records confirm. Included as one of few entries representing Oceania/Australia for this year.

events.title: Italian colonial war intensifies in Libya
events.description: Throughout 1928, Italy intensified its military campaigns to pacify and consolidate control over Libya, particularly the interior regions of Cyrenaica where Omar al-Mukhtar led a sustained guerrilla resistance against Italian forces. The Italian campaign, which would escalate dramatically under Marshal Pietro Badoglio and General Rodolfo Graziani in 1929–31, employed increasingly brutal methods including mass deportations, concentration camps, and the deliberate destruction of livestock and crops. This colonial war, poorly known outside the Arab world and Italy, resulted in the deaths of a significant portion of the Cyrenaican population.

sources.name: Italian military records; contemporary Arabic-language accounts
Ahmida, Ali Abdullatif, 'Forgotten Voices: Power and Agency in Colonial and Postcolonial Libya' (2005)

events.certainty_note: Italian military archives and Libyan oral histories document the campaign. Specific engagement details for 1928 are less granular than the broader 1923–1932 resistance narrative.

disconfirming_evidence: Several events listed above have contested elements in modern scholarship: (1) Margaret Mead's 'Coming of Age in Samoa' (1928-013): Derek Freeman's 1983 critique argued that Mead's informants had deliberately misled her about Samoan sexual behavior and that her cultural determinist conclusions were fundamentally flawed. While Freeman's own work has also been criticized for methodological issues, the Mead-Freeman debate remains unresolved, and most anthropologists now consider Mead's portrait of Samoa significantly idealized. (2) Fleming's penicillin discovery (1928-001): The narrative of pure serendipity is oversimplified. Fleming did not immediately grasp the full therapeutic potential; his 1929 paper focused more on penicillin's laboratory uses. The discovery might have remained a footnote without Florey and Chain's work over a decade later. Some historians argue Fleming receives disproportionate credit relative to Florey and Chain. (3) The Kellogg-Briand Pact (1928-002): Its characterization as either a meaningful achievement or a naive failure is debated. Recent scholarship (Hathaway & Shapiro, 2017) argues it was more consequential than the conventional 'toothless treaty' narrative suggests, as it helped establish the legal norm against conquest that underpins modern international law. (4) The 1928 Amsterdam Olympics women's 800m controversy (1928-014): The claim that women collapsed en masse after the 800m has been challenged. Contemporary accounts suggest only one or two runners showed visible distress, and the incident was exaggerated by journalists and IOC officials who were already opposed to women's athletics, providing a pretext for the subsequent ban. (5) Turkey's alphabet reform (1928-018): Geoffrey Lewis's characterization of it as a 'catastrophic success' highlights the genuine trade-off: while literacy improved, the reform created a sharp cultural discontinuity, and claims about its necessity for Turkish phonology have been questioned by some linguists who argue a modified Arabic script could have served.
historiographic_note: 1928 is richly documented across most of the globe through governmental archives, press, diplomatic correspondence, published books and journals, and photographic/early film records. However, significant biases shape the record: European and American events are disproportionately documented in accessible English-language sources; Chinese events are filtered through Nationalist, Communist, and foreign perspectives that diverge sharply; Soviet records were heavily censored and falsified by the state; colonial Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific are documented primarily through colonial administrative records that center European interests and perspectives. Indigenous and subaltern voices are generally recoverable only through later oral history projects or oblique readings of colonial documents. The interwar period's documentation is also shaped by contemporary ideological commitments — liberal internationalist, communist, fascist, and anticolonial framings all influenced what was recorded and how.

graph_edges.note: Fleming's observation ultimately led to the antibiotic revolution and his Nobel Prize in 1945
The Kellogg-Briand Pact's outlawing of aggressive war became a legal basis for prosecution at Nuremberg
Japanese assassination of Zhang Zuolin and Nationalist reunification set stage for Manchurian crisis
First Five-Year Plan's forced collectivization directly caused the catastrophic 1932-33 famine
Hoover's election preceded and was defined by the economic catastrophe that began months into his term
The Muslim Brotherhood became a major political force contributing to the instability that led to the 1952 revolution
CCP's post-1927 strategy debated at the Sixth Congress shaped the party's trajectory toward rural revolution
Salazar's rise to power in 1928 inaugurated the authoritarian system overthrown in 1974
The Jinan Incident was an early instance of direct Sino-Japanese military confrontation foreshadowing full-scale war
Stalin's 1928 moves against the Right Opposition and the Shakhty Trial model prefigured the Great Purge
Discovery of Raman effect directly led to the 1930 Nobel Prize in Physics
The 1928 Fort Vanguardia incident was a direct precursor to the full-scale Chaco War
The Northern Expedition's completion followed from the 1927 KMT-CCP split and consolidation of Nationalist power
Defeat of the Left Opposition freed Stalin to turn against the Right
The Jinan Incident occurred during the same Northern Expedition campaign that captured Beijing
